## Imperfect Human Feedback: Inconsistency {.nav-feedback}

The paper demonstrated that their enhanced model with time-inconsistency and false beliefs aligned with humans.

They asked humans $(n=50)$ to explain observed behaviors, and fit their own model to those behaviors.

<div style="text-align: center;">
![](images/ignorant.png){width="90%"}
</div>

<!-- They find empircally that there was some match in the probability distributions.

**Takeaways**: time-inconsistency and false beliefs are real, systematic ways humans are biased when providing demonstrations. Including these in reward learning models could improve the accuracy of the recovered reward. -->

Found a large similarities between model and human explanations.

[@inconsistent]{style="font-size: 0.6em;"}
